chunk_summary: This section outlines a practical approach to micronutrient risk assessment in children, focusing on clinical risk mapping to guide test selection based on age, diet, morbidity, and socioeconomic factors, particularly in the Indian context.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of clinical risk mapping in identifying micronutrient deficiencies in children.
Learn to link dietary patterns, growth stages, and morbidity profiles to potential deficiencies.
Develop decision-first testing strategies considering cost, infection, and programmatic prophylaxis in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Micronutrient deficiencies in children are often linked to diet quality, growth velocity, and social determinants.
High-risk groups in India include exclusively breastfed infants beyond 6 months without iron-rich complementary feeding, preterm or LBW infants, and children with SAM.
Decision-first testing is critical in India due to distorted biomarkers from infections and cost constraints.
Pre-test probability statements (e.g., 'microcytosis with excess milk intake') should guide test selection and follow-up.
Programmatic prophylaxis, such as iron–folic acid supplementation, may alter baseline micronutrient status.
reference_quote: High-yield risk groups in Indian practice include exclusive breastfeeding beyond 6 months without iron-rich complementary feeding, preterm or LBW infants, restrictive vegetarian diets, children with chronic diarrhea/celiac disease, and children with SAM.
document_key_topics: Clinical risk mapping for test selection
